source_title: A Conversation with Charlie Munger & John Collison - [Invest Like the Best, REPLAY]

paragraphs.speaker: patrick
paragraphs.name: Patrick O'Shaughnessy
paragraphs.text: Today, we're replaying a conversation between John Collison and Charlie Munger that we first aired on 5 December 2023. Charlie said, 'The best thing a human being can do is to help another human being know more.' He does just that in this interview. Please enjoy, and may Charlie rest in Peace.

paragraphs.speaker: john
paragraphs.name: John Collison
paragraphs.text: We are here in Charlie's house in L.A. where you have lived for 61 years, and you designed this house. I read Poor Charlie's Almanac pretty early in starting Stripe. I feel like the book is an ode to thinking for yourself. There's all this received wisdom, and it requires a certain bit of confidence to work up to actually question some of that received wisdom or come at things in your own way.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: The conventional financial world is pretty reliable if you want to use electrical engineering or automobile transportation, but in a messy world of running businesses and institutions, the conventional religion is asinine. My theory from the very beginning was I want to eliminate all the most conventional asininity. And I saw that if I could just do that, I'd have an advantage over most people.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: I early got the idea I would learn the big ideas in pretty much all the disciplines and reduce the use of those big ideas to fluency by constantly using them. That would give me an advantage in what might be called common sense. The people who are sensible on practically everything they deal with, they're uncommon, not common. Most people are a mass of crazy prejudices.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: When I was introduced to the math of Pascal and elementary probability, I saw immediately how important this math was. My math teacher had no idea that he'd come to a part of math that was very important in the practical world to everybody, but I saw it immediately and I just utterly mastered it. And I used it. I'm still using it. I used it routinely all my life quite intensely.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: If you make your living selling things to other people that are good for them, that is safer and more profitable averaged out than selling them stuff that's bad for them, like gambling, drugs, crazy religions, all kinds of things that are terrible for people. It's amazing the people who don't pay any attention to that rule.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: I wanted to work with the kind of people that I admired. Search out the reliable people that you can trust and be the kind of person dealing with everybody else that they can trust. It's just a huge advantage if you start doing that young and keep doing it consistently through life. It isn't very hard.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: The trouble with investment is most business is going to perish. Over a scale of 300 years, practically everything perishes. All the great retailers, 90% of them have perished in the last 100 years. Kodak perished, all kinds of big things that look permanent. And of course, successful investment, you want to anticipate things that are going to destroy businesses.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: In practical life, you want to succeed. You got to do two things. You got to have a certain amount of competence, and you have to know what you know and what you don't know. You have to know the edge of your competency. Better to have an IQ of 160 and think it's 150 than an IQ of 160 and think it's 200. That guy's going to kill you because he doesn't know the edge of his own competency.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: Costco requires no working capital because the turnover is so high that they don't have to pay the supplier until they've been paid by the customers. Somebody else is financing their inventories. You could run Costco with practically zero capital if you wanted to. Of course, we like a business like that a lot better than ordinary businesses.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: Costco had a membership system. There are three things that Costco didn't want. It didn't want people who stole merchandise. It didn't want people who used bad checks. It didn't want people cluttering up its goddamn parking lot without spending a hell of a lot of money in stores. Costco's always at the lowest shrink rate in the world. The net theft rate at Costco was always below two-tenths of 1%. That's unheard of.

paragraphs.speaker: charlie
paragraphs.name: Charlie Munger
paragraphs.text: Investing has gotten way harder. There's so much more of this wealth invested in securities. Way more brains came into the business. So it's gotten brutally competitive. And in my lifetime, a guy who's bought the best common stocks and sat on his ass would have made about 10% per annum before inflation, maybe 8% after inflation.
